frameworks.name: Crick's enzyme-based error correction
frameworks.one_line: enzymatic re-marking proofreads protein modification state to extend memory

frameworks.name: molecular memory switch
frameworks.one_line: protein post-translational mark as bistable memory storage element

frameworks.name: noise averaging over protein populations
frameworks.one_line: redundancy across many proteins as alternative noise-mitigation route

core_question: How can protein-mark-based molecular memories persist despite intrinsic noise and limited component lifetimes?
key_assumptions: memory is encoded in a post-translational mark state
proteins and marks have finite biophysical lifetimes
marking/demarking is a stochastic molecular process
models tractable enough to solve mostly exactly
manipulations: presence vs absence of enzymatic error correction
number of proteins storing the memory
key_findings: error correction effectively extends memory lifetime
large protein populations only marginally improve lifetime
excess error correction inflates false-positive probability
interpretations: Crick-style enzymatic proofreading is a viable principle for long-lived molecular memory
there is a trade-off where over-correction degrades fidelity
limitations_noted: analysis restricted to minimal/simplified switch models
